one_liner: A 31-year-old G2P1 woman at 26 weeks gestation presents to her prenatal visit for routine screening. She is asymptomatic but notes that her previous baby was born weighing 10 lb 2 oz.

stage2.hpi_extension: She has had an uncomplicated pregnancy so far with normal first-trimester labs. She denies polyuria, polydipsia, weight loss, or visual changes. Her prior pregnancy two years ago resulted in a macrosomic infant (4,600 g / 10 lb 2 oz) delivered by cesarean after shoulder dystocia; she was not formally screened for diabetes postpartum. Her mother has type 2 diabetes. She is here for her 24–28 week glucose screening.
stage2.surgical_history: Cesarean section two years ago for shoulder dystocia.
stage2.family_history: Mother with type 2 diabetes on metformin. Father with hypertension.
stage2.social_history: Non-smoker, no alcohol since conception. Sedentary job. Hispanic ethnicity.
stage2.physical_exam: General: well-appearing gravid female. Weight gain 18 lb so far (above target for BMI). Abdomen: gravid, fundal height 29 cm at 26 weeks (size greater than dates, concerning for macrosomia or polyhydramnios). Fetal heart tones 148 bpm. Extremities: mild dependent edema. No acanthosis nigricans noted at the nape. No signs of infection.

lab_results.result: 50-g oral glucose challenge test (non-fasting): 1-hour plasma glucose = 168 mg/dL (abnormal, >140). Confirmatory 3-hour 100-g oral glucose tolerance test (fasting): fasting 98 (normal <95), 1-hour 198 (abnormal, >180), 2-hour 170 (abnormal, >155), 3-hour 148 (abnormal, >140). Two or more values elevated = diagnostic of gestational diabetes (Carpenter-Coustan criteria).
lab_results.clinical_significance: Standard two-step screening: universal 50-g GCT at 24–28 weeks, followed by diagnostic 3-hour 100-g OGTT if the 1-hour is elevated. Two or more abnormal values on the OGTT establish the diagnosis of gestational diabetes mellitus (GDM).

lab_results.result: Na 138, K 4.1, Cr 0.6, fasting glucose 98 mg/dL, HbA1c 5.9%. Liver function normal.
lab_results.clinical_significance: HbA1c in the 'high-normal'/prediabetic range. Elevated fasting glucose and mildly elevated A1c help distinguish new-onset GDM from pre-existing (overt) type 2 diabetes that was unrecognized before pregnancy (A1c >=6.5% at first visit suggests overt DM).

lab_results.result: Obstetric ultrasound: single live intrauterine fetus, estimated fetal weight at the 92nd percentile for gestational age, polyhydramnios (AFI 26 cm). Placenta fundal, no previa. Umbilical artery Dopplers normal.
lab_results.clinical_significance: Macrosomia (large for gestational age) and polyhydramnios are classic fetal signs of maternal hyperglycemia (fetal hyperinsulinemia drives anabolism and osmotic diuresis).

stage5.final_clue: She is started on a diabetic diet and carbohydrate counting with home glucose monitoring. Despite dietary compliance, her fasting values remain above 95 mg/dL, so insulin is initiated (the preferred pharmacotherapy in pregnancy). At delivery her infant develops transient neonatal hypoglycemia from fetal hyperinsulinemia and is fed early. A 75-g 2-hour OGTT at 6–12 weeks postpartum is scheduled to screen for persistent type 2 diabetes.

debrief.classic_presentation: Gestational diabetes is glucose intolerance first recognized in pregnancy, typically in the second or third trimester. Most patients are asymptomatic and identified by universal screening. Risk factors include obesity, age >25, family history of diabetes, history of GDM or macrosomia, PCOS, and higher-risk ethnic backgrounds (Hispanic, African American, Native American, South/East Asian, Pacific Islander). Clues include a prior macrosomic infant, size-greater-than-dates uterus, and polyhydramnios.
debrief.pathophysiology: Placental hormones (human placental lactogen, estrogen, progesterone, cortisol) create a state of progressive insulin resistance through pregnancy to shunt glucose to the fetus. Most women compensate with increased pancreatic insulin secretion; those with limited beta-cell reserve develop hyperglycemia. Maternal glucose freely crosses the placenta; maternal insulin does not. The fetus responds with hyperinsulinemia, which drives macrosomia (insulin is an anabolic growth factor) and, after delivery, a sudden cessation of maternal glucose supply leads to neonatal hypoglycemia. Fetal hyperinsulinemia also delays surfactant production (increased risk of neonatal respiratory distress) and contributes to polycythemia and hyperbilirubinemia.
debrief.treatment_overview: Step 1: medical nutrition therapy (complex carbs, portion control) and moderate exercise; home glucose monitoring targeting fasting <95 and 1-hour postprandial <140. Step 2: pharmacotherapy if targets are not met — insulin is first-line (does not cross placenta). Metformin and glyburide are alternatives but cross the placenta. Deliver by 39–40 weeks if well controlled; earlier for vascular or glycemic complications. Estimate fetal weight near term; offer cesarean for estimated fetal weight >=4,500 g to reduce shoulder dystocia and brachial plexus (Erb-Duchenne) injury. Rescreen with a 75-g 2-hour OGTT at 6–12 weeks postpartum — 50% lifetime risk of type 2 diabetes.
high_yield_pearls: Universal screening at 24–28 weeks with the 50-g 1-hour glucose challenge; confirm with a 3-hour 100-g OGTT (two-step) or use a one-step 75-g 2-hour OGTT.

Fetal complications: macrosomia, shoulder dystocia, brachial plexus injury, neonatal hypoglycemia, polycythemia, hyperbilirubinemia, respiratory distress, and polyhydramnios. Unlike pregestational DM, GDM does not cause congenital anomalies because hyperglycemia begins after organogenesis.
Insulin is the preferred pharmacologic therapy in GDM because it does not cross the placenta; metformin and glyburide are alternatives.
Neonatal hypoglycemia after delivery is driven by persistent fetal hyperinsulinemia once the maternal glucose supply ends — feed early, monitor glucose.
Rescreen all GDM patients with a 75-g 2-hour OGTT at 6–12 weeks postpartum; these women carry roughly a 50% lifetime risk of developing type 2 diabetes.
sources: First Aid for the USMLE Step 1 (2017) — Reproductive chapter, 'Pregnancy complications — Gestational diabetes' section.
First Aid for the USMLE Step 1 (2017) — Endocrine chapter, 'Diabetes mellitus — types and complications' (for GDM/T2DM overlap).
